eli3: Variance measures how spread out a dataset is — specifically, the average squared distance of each value from the mean. Squaring has two effects: it makes all deviations positive, and it penalises large deviations more than small ones.
For {2, 4, 6}: mean = 4. Deviations are −2, 0, +2. Squared deviations are 4, 0, 4. Variance = (4+0+4)/3 = 2.67 (population) or (4+0+4)/2 = 4 (sample, dividing by n−1 for an unbiased estimate).

Variance is in squared units. To get back to the original units, take the square root — that gives you the standard deviation. Variance appears everywhere in statistics: it is the quantity that the CLT, ANOVA, and regression all decompose and reason about.

whenWhy.title: Measuring spread and uncertainty
whenWhy.body: Variance quantifies how much a distribution is spread around its mean. In finance, variance of returns = risk. In ML, variance of predictions across training sets = variance component of the bias–variance tradeoff. Low variance = consistent predictions; high variance = sensitivity to training data.

whenWhy.title: Variance decomposition (bias–variance tradeoff)
whenWhy.body: E[(ŷ−y)²] = Bias(ŷ)² + Var(ŷ) + σ². Understanding variance as a quantity is prerequisite to diagnosing overfitting (high variance) vs underfitting (high bias) and to explaining regularisation's effect.

whenWhy.title: ANOVA and regression
whenWhy.body: ANOVA partitions total variance into between-group and within-group components. Linear regression minimises residual variance. The coefficient of determination R² = 1 − Var(residuals)/Var(y) is the proportion of variance explained. Variance is the fundamental currency of regression analysis.

whenWhy.title: Portfolio construction (Markowitz)
whenWhy.body: Portfolio variance σ²_p = wᵀΣw, where Σ is the covariance matrix and w is the weight vector. Modern portfolio theory (Markowitz 1952) formulates the optimal portfolio as the one that minimises variance for a given expected return. Variance = risk in this framework.

deepDiveIntro: Population variance is the expected squared deviation from the mean. The sample version divides by n minus 1 rather than n — a correction that makes the estimator unbiased. Dividing by n instead gives the maximum likelihood estimator under a normal model, which systematically underestimates the population variance by a factor proportional to one over n.

Variance is only defined when the second moment of the distribution is finite. For heavy-tailed distributions such as the Cauchy, the variance is infinite and the sample variance diverges as sample size grows. In such cases, the interquartile range or median absolute deviation are more meaningful and stable measures of spread.

deepDive.heading: Population vs Sample Variance
deepDive.body: Variance measures average squared spread around the mean, but the population and sample versions are not identical. In sample data, one degree of freedom is spent estimating the mean first, which is why the usual unbiased sample variance divides by one less than the sample size. This correction matters most in smaller datasets.

deepDive.heading: Computational Formula
deepDive.body: Variance can be written in an algebraically convenient way using the mean of squares minus the square of the mean. That identity is helpful for derivations and streaming computation, but it can be numerically fragile when large nearly equal quantities are subtracted. In software, stable one-pass algorithms are usually preferred for real data.

deepDive.heading: Variance of Linear Combinations
deepDive.body: Variance behaves predictably under scaling and combination. Stretching a variable makes its variance grow quadratically, and adding variables introduces covariance terms that can either increase or decrease total spread. This is the key reason covariance matters in portfolios, ensembles, and multivariable models.

deepDive.heading: Bias–Variance Decomposition
deepDive.body: Variance is also one of the main ingredients in prediction error. In the bias-variance tradeoff, it captures how much a fitted model would change if you retrained it on a different sample from the same population. Highly flexible models often have large variance, which is why regularization and averaging methods can improve generalization.

takeaways: Population variance σ² = E[(X−μ)²]; sample variance s² = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²/(n−1) — unbiased for σ². [1]
Bessel's correction (n−1) corrects for the degree of freedom consumed by estimating x̄. [1]
σ² = E[X²] − (E[X])² — computational form, but use Welford's algorithm for numerical stability. [1]
Var(aX + b) = a²Var(X); Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y) + 2Cov(X,Y). [1]
In bias–variance tradeoff: variance = sensitivity of predictions to training set fluctuations. [2]
Variance undefined for heavy-tailed distributions (Cauchy); use IQR or MAD instead. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does dividing by n−1 (not n) give an unbiased estimate?
edgeCases.a: When we compute Σ(xᵢ−x̄)², we use x̄ instead of the true μ. Since x̄ minimises Σ(xᵢ−c)² over c, the sum Σ(xᵢ−x̄)² is always ≤ Σ(xᵢ−μ)². This systematic underestimation must be corrected. Algebraically: E[Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²] = (n−1)σ², so dividing by n−1 recovers σ². [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the difference between variance and mean squared error?
edgeCases.a: MSE(θ̂) = E[(θ̂−θ)²] = Var(θ̂) + Bias(θ̂)². Variance measures spread around the estimator's own mean E[θ̂], while MSE measures spread around the true parameter θ. For unbiased estimators (Bias = 0), MSE = Var. For biased estimators, you may accept higher bias to get lower variance — the bias–variance tradeoff. [1]

Yes. s² is both unbiased and consistent: as n→∞, s² →ₚ σ² by the Law of Large Numbers. The asymptotic distribution of s² is: √n(s² − σ²) →_d N(0, μ₄ − σ⁴), where μ₄ = E[(X−μ)⁴] is the fourth central moment. For normal data, μ₄ = 3σ⁴, so the asymptotic variance simplifies to 2σ⁴. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 2 & 7
Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman — The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
